findings.title: Database connection strings stored in plaintext ConfigMap
findings.description: The sigma1-infra-endpoints ConfigMap (subtask 1007) stores database and Redis connection URLs in plaintext. While the PostgreSQL password is intentionally omitted from the URL, the ConfigMap still exposes internal service topology, database names, and port information. More critically, the CNPG_SIGMA1_URL includes the username in plaintext. ConfigMaps are not encrypted at rest by default in Kubernetes and are readable by anyone with namespace read access.
findings.recommendation: Use Kubernetes Secrets instead of ConfigMaps for all connection strings, or at minimum ensure etcd encryption at rest is enabled (EncryptionConfiguration). Consider using External Secrets Operator to source connection details from a secrets manager rather than embedding them in cluster objects.

findings.title: GDPR internal endpoints rely on network-level enforcement with weak defense-in-depth
findings.description: All five services expose /internal/gdpr/export/:id and /internal/gdpr/delete/:id endpoints. The primary access control is CiliumNetworkPolicy (network-level), with only a thin X-Internal-Request or X-Internal-Key header check as defense-in-depth. The equipment-catalog service (subtask 2010) uses only an X-Internal-Request header presence check (not a secret value), meaning any pod within the allowed network policy can invoke GDPR deletion without authentication. A compromised pod within the allowed ingress could delete all customer data across all services.
findings.recommendation: Enforce mutual TLS (mTLS) via Cilium or a service mesh for all /internal/* endpoints. Replace the X-Internal-Request header check with a shared HMAC-signed token or mutual certificate validation. The GDPR delete operation should require a second factor — e.g., a time-limited signed JWT issued by Morgan's admin skill, not just a header presence check.

findings.title: Secrets stored as Helm values placeholders with base64 encoding only
findings.description: Subtask 1005 creates all nine Kubernetes Secrets using Helm values with base64-encoded placeholders and a values-secrets.yaml.example file. Base64 is encoding, not encryption. If the values-secrets.yaml file containing real secrets is accidentally committed to the GitOps repo, all API keys (Stripe, OpenAI, Twilio, etc.) are exposed. The plan does not mention SealedSecrets, External Secrets Operator, or any secrets management solution.
findings.recommendation: Implement SealedSecrets (Bitnami) or External Secrets Operator integrated with a secrets manager (HashiCorp Vault, AWS Secrets Manager, or Cloudflare Workers KV for secrets). Add .gitignore rules for values-secrets.yaml. The secret rotation CronJob (10012) should be upgraded from a reminder to actual automated rotation using ESO's rotation capabilities.

findings.title: JWT_SECRET shared as a single symmetric key across all services
findings.description: All services (equipment-catalog, RMS, finance, customer-vetting, social-engine) validate JWTs using the same JWT_SECRET from sigma1-jwt-secret. A compromise of any single service's environment variables exposes the signing key, allowing an attacker to forge valid JWTs for all other services including admin tokens. There is no token issuer validation, no audience claim checking, and no key rotation mechanism.
findings.recommendation: Migrate to asymmetric JWT signing (RS256/ES256) with a centralized identity provider issuing tokens. Each service validates using the public key only. Add issuer (iss) and audience (aud) claim validation per service. Implement key rotation via JWKS endpoint. As a short-term mitigation, add aud claim checks so a token issued for the catalog service cannot be used against the finance service.

findings.title: Signal-CLI uses :latest tag with no image pinning or scanning
findings.description: Subtask 1006 deploys Signal-CLI using image signalapp/signal-cli:latest. The :latest tag is mutable, unpinnable, and not from a verified publisher registry. Signal-CLI runs with a PVC containing sensitive registration data and has network access to the cluster. No container image scanning is mentioned for any service image.
findings.recommendation: Pin Signal-CLI to a specific digest (e.g., signalapp/signal-cli@sha256:...). Add container image scanning to the CI pipeline (e.g., Trivy, Grype) for all images including third-party ones. Consider building a custom Signal-CLI image from source with a verified base image. Apply the same image pinning policy to all service Dockerfiles (the Rust Dockerfile in 2011 uses debian:bookworm-slim which should also be digest-pinned).

findings.title: No explicit input validation or SQL injection protection strategy for dynamic query building
findings.description: The equipment-catalog service (subtask 2004) builds dynamic SQL queries using QueryBuilder for search and filter parameters. While sqlx provides parameterized queries, the use of QueryBuilder with dynamic WHERE clauses increases the risk of SQL injection if parameters are not consistently bound. The RMS Go service uses pgx which also supports parameterized queries, but no explicit mention of input sanitization or parameterized query enforcement is made across all services.
findings.recommendation: Establish a project-wide policy: all SQL must use parameterized queries (sqlx bind params in Rust, pgx named params in Go). Add sqlx compile-time query checking (sqlx::query! macro) where possible instead of QueryBuilder. Add integration tests that attempt SQL injection payloads on search endpoints. Consider adding a WAF rule at the Cloudflare level for common injection patterns.

findings.title: Stripe webhook signature validation implementation details are underspecified
findings.description: The finance service Stripe webhook endpoint (subtask 4005) must validate Stripe-Signature headers. The description mentions reading the raw request body before deserialization, which is correct, but the implementation detail about whether to use the stripe-rust crate's built-in verification or manual HMAC-SHA256 is left ambiguous. Incorrect implementation (e.g., deserializing before signature check, timing-attack-vulnerable comparison) is a common vulnerability.
findings.recommendation: Mandate use of the stripe-rust crate's built-in webhook::Webhook::construct_event() function which handles constant-time comparison and raw body parsing correctly. Add an explicit integration test that sends a request with a tampered body but valid-looking signature header and verifies rejection. Document the requirement to read the raw body bytes before any JSON parsing middleware.

findings.title: PII stored in multiple databases without field-level encryption
findings.description: Customer PII (names, emails, phone numbers, addresses) is stored across multiple service databases: crew_members (name, email, phone), opportunities (customer_id linkable to PII), payroll_entries (worker_name), vetting_results (company data). No field-level encryption at rest is specified. While CNPG supports TLS for connections, the data at rest in PostgreSQL is only protected by volume encryption if the storage class supports it, which is not specified.
findings.recommendation: Enable PostgreSQL Transparent Data Encryption (TDE) if supported by CNPG, or ensure the underlying storage class uses encrypted volumes (e.g., encrypted EBS/PD). For highly sensitive fields (credit_score, risk_flags, worker payment amounts), implement application-level encryption using pgcrypto's pgp_sym_encrypt/decrypt with a key managed outside the database. Add encryption-at-rest validation to the infrastructure test suite.

findings.title: Valkey deployed without authentication in dev and no production auth requirement enforced
findings.description: Subtask 1004 explicitly states 'no auth for dev (document production auth requirement)' for Valkey. However, the production hardening task (Task 10) scales Valkey to 2 replicas but does not include a subtask to enable authentication. Any pod that gains network access to Valkey can read/write all cached data including vetting results, currency rates, and rate limit state.
findings.recommendation: Add a subtask to Task 10 that enables Valkey AUTH with a strong password stored in a Kubernetes Secret. Update all service Redis connection strings to include the password. Ensure the CiliumNetworkPolicy for Valkey allows connections only from the five application services.

findings.title: CiliumNetworkPolicy allows Morgan egress to all services without granular port restrictions
findings.description: The Morgan CiliumNetworkPolicy (subtask 10009) allows egress to all 5 backend services. Since Morgan is internet-facing (via Cloudflare Tunnel), a compromised Morgan pod could be used as a pivot point to access any backend service endpoint, including GDPR delete endpoints and admin write endpoints.
findings.recommendation: Restrict Morgan's egress policies to specific ports and paths where possible. Use Cilium L7 policy to limit Morgan to only the specific HTTP paths it needs (e.g., /api/v1/* and /internal/gdpr/* but not /metrics or /health/ready). Add rate limiting at the Cilium layer for Morgan's outbound connections to prevent a compromised agent from performing DoS against backend services.

findings.title: No security event logging or audit trail beyond GDPR deletions
findings.description: The only audit logging mentioned is for GDPR deletion operations (writing to the audit schema). There is no audit trail for: admin JWT token usage, failed authentication attempts, secret access, configuration changes, or Morgan's tool invocations. Without security event logging, incident detection and forensic analysis are severely limited.
findings.recommendation: Add structured security event logging to all services: log all authentication failures (invalid JWT, expired token), all admin-level operations (product creation, invoice send), all GDPR operations, and all Morgan tool invocations with the invoking user/session. Ship these logs to a dedicated security index in Loki with a longer retention period. Add alerting rules for: >10 auth failures per minute, any GDPR delete operation, admin token usage from unexpected sources.

findings.title: No dependency pinning or audit strategy specified for Node.js and Go services
findings.description: While Rust services use Cargo.lock for deterministic builds, the Node.js social-engine (Task 6) and Go RMS (Task 3) services do not mention lockfile strategies. The CI security pipeline (subtask 10015 area) mentions cargo-audit and npm audit but the expanded task details are incomplete. No mention of Go vulnerability scanning (govulncheck) or SBOM generation.
findings.recommendation: Mandate committed lockfiles: package-lock.json or bun.lockb for Node.js, go.sum for Go (already standard). Add govulncheck to the Go CI pipeline alongside golangci-lint. Add SBOM generation (syft or cyclonedx) as a CI step for all services. Pin all dependency versions to exact versions (not ranges) in package.json. Add Dependabot or Renovate for automated dependency update PRs.

findings.title: Rate limiter uses client IP which can be spoofed behind proxies
findings.description: The equipment-catalog rate limiter (subtask 2006) extracts client IP from ConnectInfo<SocketAddr>. Behind Cloudflare Tunnel and potential reverse proxies, the direct socket address will be the proxy's IP, not the client's. This means all requests through the same proxy share a rate limit bucket, or an attacker can bypass rate limiting by connecting from different proxies.
findings.recommendation: Extract the real client IP from the CF-Connecting-IP or X-Forwarded-For header (with Cloudflare's IP ranges validated). Use the tenant_id from JWT claims as the primary rate limit key when available, falling back to the validated client IP for unauthenticated requests. Add the trusted proxy configuration to the Axum ConnectInfo setup.

findings.title: CORS configured as permissive for dev with no production tightening task
findings.description: Multiple services (equipment-catalog in 2002, social-engine web chat in 7007) configure CORS as permissive ('*' origins) for development. The production hardening task does not include a subtask to tighten CORS policies to only allow the sigma1.com domain and the mobile app's origin.
findings.recommendation: Add a production hardening subtask to update CORS configuration on all services to allow only: https://sigma1.com, https://www.sigma1.com, https://chat.sigma1.com, and the mobile app's custom scheme. Configure CORS as environment-variable-driven so dev can remain permissive while production is locked down.

findings.title: Database connections do not enforce TLS between application pods and PostgreSQL
findings.description: The CNPG_SIGMA1_URL connection string in the ConfigMap uses a plain postgresql:// URL with no sslmode parameter. CloudNativePG supports TLS but it must be explicitly configured in both the Cluster CR and the connection string. Without TLS, database credentials and query data travel in plaintext within the cluster network, vulnerable to network sniffing by any pod with host network access.
findings.recommendation: Configure the CNPG Cluster CR with spec.postgresql.pg_hba entries requiring TLS (hostssl). Update all connection strings to include sslmode=verify-full with the CNPG-generated CA certificate mounted as a volume in each service pod. Add a test that verifies connections without TLS are rejected.

findings.title: OpenClaw Morgan agent has unrestricted access to all service write endpoints
findings.description: Morgan's MCP tool configuration (subtask 7003) provides direct HTTP access to create invoices, create opportunities, trigger vetting runs, publish social posts, and execute GDPR deletions. There is no per-tool authorization boundary — Morgan's single JWT or service identity can invoke any operation. If the AI agent hallucinates or is prompt-injected, it could create fraudulent invoices, delete customer data, or publish unauthorized content.
findings.recommendation: Implement tool-level authorization controls: 1) Morgan should use scoped JWTs with specific permissions per tool (e.g., finance:create_invoice, gdpr:delete). 2) High-risk operations (GDPR delete, invoice creation above a threshold, social media publishing) should require human-in-the-loop confirmation via Signal before execution. 3) Add rate limits on Morgan's tool invocations (e.g., max 5 GDPR deletes per hour). 4) Log all tool invocations with full request/response payloads to the audit schema.

strengths: CiliumNetworkPolicies provide strong network-level micro-segmentation between all services with explicit allow rules and default deny
GDPR compliance is architecturally embedded — every service has export and delete endpoints, with a centralized orchestration layer in Morgan
PostgreSQL schema-level isolation with per-service roles (sigma1_catalog, sigma1_rms, etc.) follows the principle of least privilege at the database layer
Health check endpoints are consistently implemented across all services with both liveness and readiness probes, enabling rapid detection of degraded states
The CI/CD pipeline includes security scanning (Semgrep, cargo-audit, npm audit) as a merge gate, preventing known vulnerabilities from reaching production
Stripe webhook signature validation is explicitly called out as a requirement with the correct approach of raw body reading before deserialization

Background vetting pipeline implements graceful degradation — individual step failures set risk flags rather than failing the entire pipeline, preventing denial-of-service through external API outages
Rate limiting is implemented at the application level using Valkey sliding windows with appropriate per-tenant scoping
summary: The Sigma-1 platform has a solid security foundation with CiliumNetworkPolicies, per-schema database isolation, and embedded GDPR compliance. However, several high-severity gaps require attention before production: the shared symmetric JWT secret creates a single point of compromise across all services, GDPR deletion endpoints lack strong authentication beyond network policy, secrets management relies on plaintext Helm values without a proper secrets manager, and database connections do not enforce TLS. The Morgan AI agent's unrestricted access to all service endpoints via MCP tools represents a significant risk surface that should be mitigated with scoped permissions and human-in-the-loop controls for destructive operations.
